1: ʾēl šadday — "God Almighty." The divine name ʾēl šadday appears here for the first time since 35:11 and is the characteristic name for God in the patriarchal narratives (17:1; 28:3; 35:11; 43:14; 48:3; 49:25; Exod 6:3 identifies it as the pre-Mosaic name alongside which YHWH was not fully disclosed). The etymology of šadday is disputed: BDB derives it from šādad (to overpower); Albright proposed the Akkadian šadû (mountain); another reading connects it to šēḏ (breast/nurture) suggesting divine sufficiency. The LXX consistently renders it pantokrator (almighty), which the tradition follows. Hithallek lĕpānay — "walk before me" — the Hithpael of hālaḵ, as in Enoch (5:22) and Noah (6:9); the reflexive stem indicates continuous, habitual, reciprocal movement in God's presence. Tāmîm — "blameless/complete" — not sinless perfection but moral integrity (cf. Noah in 6:9; Job 1:1; Ps 15:2).
2: ûnĕtannāh bĕrîtî — "and I will give/place my covenant." The verb here is nātan (to give), not the kārat (to cut) of chapter 15. In 15:18 YHWH "cut" (kārat) the covenant with Abram in the ceremony of passing through the animal pieces. Here the covenant is being "given" — its content specified and formalized in circumcision. The semantic difference: kārat bĕrît = the establishment-event; nātan bĕrît = the ongoing giving/granting of covenant relationship. Wĕʾarbeh ʾōtĕḵā bimĕʾōḏ mĕʾōḏ — "and I will multiply you greatly" — the double superlative mĕʾōḏ mĕʾōḏ ("very very much") is an emphatic intensifier; Hebrew uses reduplication for emphasis where English would use "exceedingly" or "greatly."
3: wayyippōl ʾaḇrām ʿal-pānāyw — "Abram fell on his face." The prostration posture (nāpal ʿal-pānāyw) is the standard response to theophany in the patriarchal narratives (17:3,17; cf. Josh 5:14; Ezek 1:28; Dan 8:17). The narrative then presents a dialogue structured by the "as for me... as for you" (waʾănî... wĕʾattāh) formula in vv4 and 9 — a covenant suzerain-vassal form where the sovereign states his obligations first and then the vassal's obligations follow. The same structure governs Exod 6:6-7 and Deut 4-5.
4: ʾāḇ hāmôn gôyim — "father of a multitude of nations." The phrase is a folk etymology for ʾaḇrāhām — the narrator connects hāmôn (multitude, crowd, noise) to the name. The historical etymology of Abraham is disputed; it may simply be a longer dialectal variant of ʾaḇrām meaning "exalted father" (in Amorite personal names, both forms exist). The covenant formula ʾănî hinnēh ("behold, I") places God as the initiating party — the covenant obligation runs from God to Abraham first.
5: wĕlōʾ yiqqārēʾ ʿôḏ ʾet-šimĕḵā ʾaḇrām wĕhāyāh šimĕḵā ʾaḇrāhām — "No longer will your name be called Abram, but your name will be Abraham." In the ANE, renaming by a superior marked a change in identity, status, and vocation (Pharaoh renames Joseph in 41:45; Nebuchadnezzar renames Daniel in Dan 1:7). The change from ʾaḇrām to ʾaḇrāhām is lexically minor — an inserted hēh — but the narrator supplies the theological freight: "father of many nations." The same pattern governs the renaming of Jacob to Israel (32:28; 35:10) and Simon to Peter (John 1:42). Kî ʾāḇ hāmôn gôyim nĕtattîḵā — the perfect tense "I have made you" (nĕtattîḵā) treats the future promise as already accomplished from God's perspective — a divine performative: the declaration creates the reality it names.
6: wĕhiprêtî ʾōtĕḵā bimĕʾōḏ mĕʾōḏ — "I will make you very, very fruitful." Pārāh (to be fruitful) echoes the creation blessing of 1:28 ("be fruitful and multiply") — the covenant promise resumes and particularizes the creation mandate in one family. Ûmĕlāḵîm mimmĕḵā yēṣēʾû — "and kings will come from you" — the royal dimension of the Abrahamic promise anticipates the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 7; cf. Gen 49:10 — the royal scepter in Judah). The three levels of the promise in v6 — fruitfulness, nations, kings — unfold in the structure of biblical history: the patriarchs become Israel (fruitfulness), Israel becomes a kingdom (nations), and David's line becomes the royal covenant (kings).
7: bĕrît ʿôlām — "everlasting covenant." ʿôlām (H5769) — long duration, from the distant past or future; in covenantal contexts it means "perpetual, ongoing, not-to-be-dissolved" rather than strictly "eternal" in a philosophical sense. The phrase bĕrît ʿôlām is used for the Noahic covenant (9:16), the Abrahamic covenant (17:7,13,19), the covenant of circumcision (17:13), the Sinai-Sabbath (Exod 31:16), the Davidic covenant (2 Sam 23:5), and the new covenant (Isa 55:3; 61:8; Jer 32:40). Lihyôt lĕḵā lēʾlōhîm — "to be to you as God" — the covenant formula; the same formulation as Exod 6:7 and throughout Deuteronomy. The bilateral structure of the covenant: God gives himself to Abraham; Abraham and seed give themselves to God.
8: ʾereṣ mĕgûreyḵā / ʾaḥuzzat ʿôlām — "the land of your sojourning" → "everlasting possession." Māgôr (sojourning, temporary residence) — Abraham is a resident alien in Canaan, not an owner. The promise is that this temporary dwelling-place will become permanent inheritance. ʾAḥuzzāh (possession, holding) — a legal term for real property; used throughout Leviticus for land rights. The tension between māgôr (sojourner) and ʾaḥuzzāh (permanent possession) generates the theological theme Hebrews exploits: the patriarchs remained sojourners even after the promise, "looking forward to the city that has foundations" (Heb 11:10,13-16).
9: wĕʾattāh ʾet-bĕrîtî tišmōr — "And as for you, you shall keep my covenant." The "as for you" (wĕʾattāh) marks the vassal's obligation in the covenant structure begun in v4 ("as for me"). Šāmar (to keep, guard, observe) — the standard term for covenant fidelity in Deuteronomy (Deut 4:2; 5:1,29; 6:2 etc.). The keeping of the covenant is not a condition for its establishment — God has already established it — but the expression of belonging to it. Verse 9 addresses Abraham directly; v10 extends to "you and your descendants," framing circumcision as the covenant's visible sign across generations.
10: zōʾt bĕrîtî... himmôl lākem kol-zāḵār — "This is my covenant... every male among you shall be circumcised." The verb mûl (H4135) is the dedicated term for circumcision; the broader verb kārat (to cut) is used for covenant-cutting but also for being "cut off" in v14. The sign of the covenant is enacted by the same root action — cutting — that established the covenant. The rite is not unique to Israel (Egyptian, Canaanite, and other ANE peoples practiced circumcision), but here it is given a distinctive covenantal meaning: marking the body as belonging to the covenant lineage. In the NT this distinctive covenantal significance is transferred to baptism (Col 2:11-12) and relocated from the body to the heart (Deut 10:16; 30:6; Jer 4:4; Rom 2:28-29).
11: ûnĕmalttem ʾēt bĕśar ʿorlĕtem wĕhāyāh lĕʾôt bĕrît bênî ûbênêkem — "You shall circumcise the flesh of your foreskins, and it shall be the sign of the covenant between me and you." ʿOrlāh (foreskin) — later takes on figurative meanings: "uncircumcised lips" (Exod 6:12), "uncircumcised ear" (Jer 6:10), "uncircumcised heart" (Lev 26:41; Jer 9:26; Ezek 44:9). ʾÔt bĕrît — "sign of the covenant" — ʾôt (H226) as covenant sign follows the rainbow (9:12-13) and will precede the blood on doorposts (Exod 12:13) and Sabbath (Exod 31:13,17). Each covenant sign is physical, visible, recurring, and carries the promise it signifies.
12: ûben-šĕmōnat yāmîm yimmôl lākem kol-zāḵār lĕdōrōtêkem — "At eight days old every male among you shall be circumcised for your generations." The eighth day is specified without explicit rationale in the text. Later tradition noted that eighth-day circumcision follows completion of one full seven-day cycle (including one Sabbath), entering a new cycle of seven. Medically, blood coagulation factors (vitamin K and prothrombin) reach optimal levels around day eight — a convergence the ancient text does not explain but that later readers have noted. The two categories yĕlîḏ bayit (born in the household) and miknāt kesep (bought with silver/money) include the entire household regardless of ethnic origin — the covenant encompasses all who dwell within its boundaries, anticipating the ingathering of the nations.
13: himmôl yimmôl yĕlîḏ bêtĕḵā ûmiknāt kaspĕḵā — "He must certainly be circumcised, the one born in your household and the one bought with your silver." The emphatic doubled verb (Niphal infinitive absolute + Niphal imperfect) himmôl yimmôl expresses absolute certainty and obligation — "he shall certainly be circumcised." Wĕhāyĕtāh bĕrîtî biḇśarkem lĕḇĕrît ʿôlām — "and my covenant shall be in your flesh as an everlasting covenant" — bĕśar (flesh) is the location of the covenant sign; the covenant is written not on stone tablets (which come later) but in the body.
14: wĕnĕkrĕtāh hannepeš hahiʾ mēʿammehā — "that person shall be cut off from his people." The wordplay here is dense: the covenant is established by cutting (kārat, 15:18); its sign is cutting (mûl, vv10-13); and those who refuse the cutting are themselves cut off (kārat in the Niphal, here). The vocabulary of covenant-making and covenant-breaking uses the same root — the one who refuses the covenant cut is subjected to the covenant curse cut. Hannepeš hahiʾ — "that person/soul" — nepeš (H5315) here = individual person, not disembodied soul; the noun functions as a demonstrative pronoun equivalent: "that one." The phrase kārat min-hāʿām (cut off from the people) is the formula for the most severe civil-religious penalty in the Mosaic law, applied to dozens of infractions in Leviticus and Numbers.
15: šārāy ʾišĕtĕḵā lōʾ-tiqrāʾ ʾet-šĕmāh šārāy kî śārāh šĕmāh — "As for Sarai your wife, you shall not call her name Sarai, but Sarah shall be her name." Both forms mean "princess/noblewoman" — śārāy is an archaic or dialectal form; śārāh is the standard form. Unlike the Abram→Abraham change, the semantic content does not shift; but the name renewal participates in the broader theme of her new identity as "mother of nations" in v16. The renaming is paired — Abraham and Sarah are renamed together — signaling that both partners are equally encompassed in the covenant promise of descendants.
16: ûḇēraḵtî ʾōtāh wĕgam nātatî mimmennāh lĕḵā bēn — "I will bless her and moreover I will give you a son by her." The gam ("also, moreover") emphasizes the addition to the promise — not just blessing in general but specifically a son through Sarah. Wĕhāyĕtāh lĕgôyim malkê ʿammîm mimmennāh yihyû — "she shall become nations; kings of peoples shall come from her." The phrase malkê ʿammîm ("kings of peoples") distinguishes a broader category than "kings of Israel" — the promise is explicitly universal. Sarah's role is not merely to bear Isaac but to be the mother of a lineage that reaches all peoples through the covenant chain.
17: wayyippōl ʾaḇrāhām ʿal-pānāyw wayyiṣḥāq wayyōʾmer bĕlibbô — "Abraham fell on his face and laughed; he said in his heart." Wayyiṣḥāq — "he laughed" — the verb ṣāḥaq is the root from which Isaac's name (yiṣḥāq) derives. But Abraham's laughter is "in his heart" (bĕlibbô) — interior, not a public repudiation. The narrator does not code the laughter negatively as he does Sarah's in 18:15 (which is rebuked); Abraham's laughter appears to be wonder and joy at the sheer magnitude of the promise rather than unbelief. The distinction matters for how the name "Isaac" functions: the one born from laughter vindicates the laugher.
18: lû yišmāʿēl yihyeh lĕpāneyḵā — "O that Ishmael might live before you!" The optative particle lû expresses a wish or longing ("O that! If only!"); this is Abraham's intercession for Ishmael — he wants the existing son to receive the covenant blessing. The phrase yihyeh lĕpāneyḵā ("live before you") parallels the covenant formula of v1 ("walk before me") — Abraham wishes Ishmael the same covenant life that God offered him. God's response in vv19-20 is not a rejection of Ishmael but a distinction: Ishmael will be blessed greatly, but the covenant (bĕrît) will pass through Isaac.
19: ûqārāʾtā ʾet-šĕmô yiṣḥāq — "you shall call his name Isaac." The name yiṣḥāq = "he laughs/he will laugh" — a Qal imperfect third person masculine singular of ṣāḥaq. The name commemorates both Abraham's laughter (17:17) and Sarah's (18:12) — the child of impossible promise is literally named after the incredulous laughter of his parents. Wahăqimōtî ʾet-bĕrîtî ʾittô lĕḇĕrît ʿôlām — "I will establish my covenant with him as an everlasting covenant." The verb qûm (Hiphil, to cause to stand/establish) is used for formally ratifying or upholding an existing covenant — God does not cut a new covenant with Isaac but establishes/confirms the one already cut with Abraham in ch 15.
20: ûlĕyišmāʿēl šĕmaʿtîḵā hinnēh bēraḵtî ʾōtô — "And as for Ishmael, I have heard you; behold, I have blessed him." Šāmaʿ (to hear) is the root of yišmāʿēl — "God hears" — so "I have heard you" is a double entendre: God hears Abraham's prayer for Ishmael, and the name Ishmael itself encapsulates this hearing. Šĕnêm-ʿāśār nĕśîʾim yôlîḏ — "he will father twelve princes" — a deliberately symmetrical promise: twelve princes for Ishmael (25:13-16) mirrors twelve sons/tribes for Israel. The parallel structure shows that Ishmael's blessing is generous and structured, even while the covenant passes through Isaac.
21: wĕʾet-bĕrîtî ʾāqîm ʾet-yiṣḥāq — "but my covenant I will establish with Isaac." The contrastive wĕʾet (but, and as for) sets the covenant in sharp distinction from the blessing: Ishmael receives the blessing (v20); Isaac receives the covenant. The verb qāyam (Hiphil of qûm) — "establish" — is applied specifically to the covenant with Isaac, not to Ishmael's great nation promise. The distinction between being blessed (bāraḵ) and being the covenant heir is the theological center of the election argument Paul uses in Romans 9:6-9 and Galatians 4:21-31.
22: wayyaʿal ʾĕlōhîm mēʿal ʾaḇrāhām — "God went up from Abraham." The language of divine ascent wayyaʿal (Qal imperfect of ʿālāh, to go up) implies that God's presence had come down to Abraham during the vision — the theophany ends with YHWH ascending as Abraham had prostrated himself during the encounter (vv3,17). The abrupt ending signals that the covenant content has been fully communicated — no lingering, no further discussion; the word of God accomplishes its purpose and the divine presence withdraws until the next encounter (ch 18).
23: bayyôm hazzeh — "on that very day." The phrase bĕʿeṣem hayyôm hazzeh ("on the bone of this day" = "on the very same day") is an emphatic temporal formula marking immediate, complete obedience. The same phrase marks the Exodus departure (Exod 12:17,41,51) and the Day of Atonement prohibitions (Lev 23:28-30). Abraham circumcises the entire household — Ishmael, the purchased servants, the home-born — on the same day God commanded it. The narrative emphasizes completeness: all males, all categories, immediate action. The obedience is as comprehensive as the command.
24: wĕʾaḇrāhām ben-tišʿîm wĕtēšaʿ šānāh bĕhimmōlô — "and Abraham was ninety-nine years old when he circumcised himself." The narrator records Abraham's age (99) and Ishmael's age (13) explicitly — the ages matter because they establish that circumcision for Abraham was a covenant-renewal act (not the rite of infancy), while Ishmael's circumcision at 13 pre-dates puberty but follows his naming and recognition as Abraham's son. The verb bĕhimmōlô (Niphal infinitive construct of mûl) — "in his being circumcised" — treats the act as passive: he was circumcised, not he circumcised himself; but verse 23 makes clear Abraham initiated it. The Niphal here emphasizes the state achieved rather than the act performed.
25: wĕyišmāʿēl bĕnô ben-šĕlōš-ʿeśrēh šānāh bĕhimmōlô — "And his son Ishmael was thirteen years old when he was circumcised." The age thirteen for Ishmael became significant in later Jewish tradition: the Mishnah and later halakha designate thirteen as the age of religious obligation (bar mitzvah). Whether the Genesis text intends this is uncertain, but the coincidence means that in later reading, Ishmael's circumcision at puberty became the type for the age of adult covenant responsibility. In the genealogy of 25:12-18, Ishmael's twelve sons represent a full legitimate nation — his circumcision at 13 includes him in the Abrahamic covenant sign even though the covenant itself passes to Isaac.
26: bĕʿeṣem hayyôm hazzeh nimmôl ʾaḇrāhām wĕyišmāʿēl bĕnô — "On that very day Abraham and his son Ishmael were circumcised." The formula bĕʿeṣem hayyôm hazzeh repeats from v23 — emphasizing simultaneity and completeness. Father and son circumcised on the same day — the covenant sign spans both generations immediately. The narrative does not record Sarah's reaction or any other response; the focus is entirely on Abraham's complete and immediate obedience. Heb 11:17-19 will later describe Abraham's obedience as grounded in the conviction that God can raise from the dead.
27: wĕkol-ʾanĕšê bêtô yĕlîḏ bayit ûmiknāt-kesep mēʾēt ben-nēḵār nimmōlû ʾittô — "And every male of his household, born in the house and bought with silver from a foreigner, were circumcised with him." The phrase ben-nēḵār (son of a foreigner/stranger) confirms that the covenant sign extends to non-Israelites within Abraham's household. The covenant community is not defined purely by blood descent but by household membership and covenant sign. This breadth anticipates the "mixed multitude" of the Exodus (Exod 12:38) and the inclusion of sojourners in Israel's covenant life (Lev 17:8-9; Num 15:14-16).
